title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
Welcome to the ship’s training manual for media consumers. This single course combines the key lessons from both modules into one navigable voyage.


In this lesson we sail through the scattered seas of paid media platforms.



Original content is spread across many different islands.
Fans may need multiple subscriptions just to access one story or album.
Manga, music, TV, and film are often split across separate services.





Pirates see the web as a broken archipelago where travelers are forced to visit too many ports.
This lesson explains why fragmentation encourages some people to look for a single unified route.





What happens when content is locked behind many gates?
How does this change a viewer’s choice between paying or pirating?





A pirate ship needs a budget. This lesson explores how piracy is often justified as a money-saving measure.



Many people turn to piracy to avoid subscription fees and premium costs.
Until the cost is negligible compared to income, pirated media can feel more affordable.
Examples include fans who want access to costly music catalogs or serialized manga.





Piracy can push the market to lower prices and offer better access.
We also talk about why this is not a simple justification for every choice.





What is the difference between saving money and taking something unfairly?
How do prices shape creative choices for consumers?





This section looks at piracy as a way of preserving content and granting access to censored media.



Some pirate communities preserve rare or old media that might otherwise disappear.
The idea of an archive like “anna’s archive” shows how people want to save information for future generations.





In places like Russia and China, some media is restricted or censored.
Piracy can be presented as a way to access art and news that would otherwise be hidden.
We examine the risks and ethics of seeking access through unregulated sources.





List a work that might be hard to access legally in a restrictive region.
Discuss whether archiving it is preservation or piracy.





In this lesson we explore arguments against piracy from the perspective of fairness and law.



People who pay for subscriptions expect the system to be fair.
When others pirate, it can feel like law-abiding customers are punished with higher fees.
This section discusses why fairness matters in a shared marketplace.





Legal media providers rely on trust and predictable revenue.
Piracy can erode that trust and make honest consumers question the value of paying.





Is it fair for legal customers to subsidize others who pirate?
How should a market balance access with support for creators?





This lesson covers the risks of piracy, from malware to untrustworthy sites.



Piracy sites are unregulated and can be dangerous.
Viruses, scams, and fake downloads are common hazards for visitors.





Pirating content may seem easy, but the hidden costs can be severe.
Users may lose data, get infected, or inadvertently support shady operations.





How can you tell whether a site is safe?
Should avoiding risk influence your decision to use pirated media?





This lesson examines why pirate sites themselves need income and what that means for the ecosystem.



Piracy sites still require money to stay online.
They often use advertising, donations, and risky monetization models.
Some sites even promote gambling or shady financial schemes.





Earning revenue from piracy can create incentives to keep the system alive.
This raises questions about whether piracy is a harmless alternative or part of a larger problem.





If a site depends on piracy income, how does that affect its ethics?
What alternatives exist for supporting access without harming creators?

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Piracy is often framed as a consumer issue, but creators sit at the ethical core of the debate
Creators invest time, money, and creative labor into their work – piracy directly affects the return on that investment
The ethical question: does the public’s interest in access outweigh the creator’s right to control and profit from their work?
Two distinct categories of rights apply: economic rights (the right to profit from your work) and moral rights (the right to control how your work is used and attributed), both recognised under the Berne Convention (World Intellectual Property Organization 1886)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Piracy is often framed as a consumer issue, but creators sit at the ethical core of the debate
Creators invest time, money, and creative labor into their work – piracy directly affects the return on that investment
The ethical question: does the public’s interest in access outweigh the creator’s right to control and profit from their work?
Two distinct categories of rights apply: economic rights (the right to profit from your work) and moral rights (the right to control how your work is used and attributed), both recognised under the Berne Convention (World Intellectual Property Organization 1886)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Economic Rights vs. Moral Rights

Copyright grants creators two types of rights under the Berne Convention (Article 6bis) (World Intellectual Property Organization 1886):

Economic rights: the right to be compensated for use of one’s work; these can be transferred or licensed
Moral rights: the right to claim authorship and object to modifications that harm reputation; these cannot be transferred or waived

Piracy primarily undermines economic rights, but can also affect moral rights (e.g., modified or cracked versions of a work distributed without the creator’s consent) (Wikipedia contributors 2024b)
The US copyright tradition is rooted almost entirely in economic rights, while the European/French tradition (“droits d’auteur”) places greater weight on moral rights – this shapes how different legal systems treat piracy (Wikipedia contributors 2024b)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Ethical Frameworks
Three established philosophical frameworks illuminate why this debate is genuinely contested rather than easily resolved:

Locke’s labour theory of property provides the philosophical foundation of copyright itself: when a person mixes their labour with raw material, the result becomes their property (Locke 1689). A game developer who spends years writing code and designing systems has mixed their labour with ideas; the resulting work is rightfully theirs. Piracy takes that product without consent – a violation of a natural property right, not merely a legal one.
Kantian deontology offers two independent reasons why piracy is ethically problematic regardless of consequences (UBC Allard School of Law – Social Media Law 2024):

Universalisability: if everyone pirated, creators would lose the income to keep creating – the very content being pirated would cease to exist. Piracy cannot be universalised without undermining itself.
Treating persons as ends: using a creator’s work purely for one’s own benefit, without consent or compensation, treats the creator as a means rather than as an agent whose rights deserve respect.

Utilitarianism is where the debate becomes most contested. The standard justification for copyright is that limited monopoly rights incentivise creation, maximising long-run social welfare (UBC Allard School of Law – Social Media Law 2024). But Waldfogel’s post-Napster analysis directly complicates this: music supply did not decline even as revenue fell sharply after file-sharing became widespread (Waldfogel 2012). If creative output is resilient to piracy, the utilitarian case for strict enforcement weakens – and freer access might produce greater total welfare by reaching more people.
These frameworks pull in different directions: Lockean natural rights and Kantian duty point toward strong creator protection, while utilitarian outcomes challenge whether that protection is actually necessary to sustain creative production. The sections below trace how each plays out in practice.

title: The Creator’s Perspective
section: The Case Against Piracy: Harm to Creators
text:
The Case Against Piracy: Harm to Creators

Metallica v. Napster (2000): the landmark creator-vs-piracy case. After discovering an unreleased demo circulating on Napster, Metallica identified 335,435 individual users sharing their catalogue and demanded they all be banned. Drummer Lars Ulrich testified before the US Senate, arguing that Napster “never asked us or other artists if we wanted to participate in their business” (Wikipedia contributors 2024a). This is a Kantian argument at its core: Napster used artists’ work as a means without seeking their consent, regardless of whether revenue was actually lost. The case forced Napster into shutdown and shaped digital copyright law for a decade.
Lost income: pirated copies represent potential sales that never materialise; most evidence suggests file sharing does displace media sales (Waldfogel 2012) – the Lockean case made concrete: the creator’s labour produces something of value, and piracy takes it without consent or compensation
Indie creators are disproportionately hurt: major studios can absorb losses, but small teams rely on every purchase to fund continued development and future projects (GuardingPearSoftware, n.d.)

tinyBuild found that their game Punch Club saw a surge in Brazilian piracy only after they launched a Portuguese localisation – users had waited for a translated version rather than paying for the English original (MCV/DEVELOP, n.d.)

Reduced incentive to innovate: with limited revenue, studios gravitate toward proven formulas rather than experimental work – empirical research on film confirms that piracy “reduces creative incentives” and negatively impacts the quality of new productions (Initiative for Digital Entertainment Analytics, Carnegie Mellon University, n.d.)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
section: The Case For Piracy: Access and Distribution
text:
The Case For Piracy: Access and Distribution

Censorship and geo-restrictions: when content is banned or refused classification, piracy becomes the only option for consumers

Australia’s Classification Board can issue a “Refused Classification” (RC) verdict, banning a game from retail and eliminating all legal distribution channels (Strickland 2019); roughly one in four Australians reported pirating online content in 2022 (SBS News 2023)
Hotline Miami 2 (2015) was refused classification in Australia; developer Jonatan Söderström publicly told Australian fans to “just pirate it” – a case where the creator acknowledged the legal system left consumers no legitimate alternative (Engadget 2015)

DRM punishes legal buyers more than pirates: CD Projekt Red removed all DRM from their games, arguing copy protection “makes the life of a legal customer more difficult” while pirates bypass it on day one anyway (Lomas 2016) – a utilitarian calculation: the harm imposed on legitimate buyers outweighs any protection gained

The Witcher 3 sold nearly 10 million copies across platforms despite being released without copy protection (Purchese 2016)
Co-founder Marcin Iwiński: “We believe in the carrot, not the stick. We cannot force people to buy things – we can only convince them to do it.” (Lomas 2016)

Piracy as a path to legitimate consumption: in lower-income regions, young people who pirate games often become paying customers once they have disposable income (Purchese 2016)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Ethical Tensions and Nuances

Does creator size change the moral calculus? Pirating from a billion-dollar studio is structurally different from pirating from a two-person indie team, even if the act is the same (GuardingPearSoftware, n.d.) – this intuition sits uneasily with Kantian ethics, where the wrongness of an act is independent of the target’s wealth or market position
When creators endorse piracy: the Hotline Miami 2 case shows the creator/pirate relationship is not always adversarial – sometimes creators view piracy as preferable to censorship (Strickland 2019)
The post-Napster paradox: the recorded music industry saw dramatic revenue decline after Napster, yet the quantity of critically recognised new music did not decline – raising questions about what “harm to creators” actually means in practice (Waldfogel 2012)
When enforcement itself causes harm: Nintendo represents the opposite extreme from CD Projekt Red – aggressively removing fan games, issuing around 20,000 GitHub takedown requests per year, and claiming millions of YouTube videos through Content ID (Nintendo Life 2024). The justification is a moral rights argument: creators must control how their work is used to protect integrity and reputation. But critics argue this chills the fan creativity that sustains Nintendo’s cultural reach. Moral rights, taken to their logical conclusion, can become a tool that harms the broader creative ecosystem they were designed to protect.
Moral rights and piracy: distributing modified or cracked versions without attribution infringes moral rights even when it causes no direct economic harm (Wikipedia contributors 2024b)

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Conclusion
The creator’s perspective on piracy resists a simple verdict. Lockean property rights and Kantian respect for consent both support strong creator protection, and the Metallica case and indie developer stories show that real harm occurs – particularly where economic survival is at stake. Yet the utilitarian evidence complicates the picture: creative supply proved resilient even after Napster, and cases like Hotline Miami 2 and CD Projekt Red show that creators themselves sometimes find piracy preferable to the alternatives on offer. The morally relevant factor may not be piracy per se, but context: who is pirating, from whom, under what conditions, and with what legitimate alternatives available. A framework that treats all piracy as equivalent – whether from a teenager in a low-income country or a bulk commercial operation – misses the ethical distinctions that actually matter.

title: Digital Piracy
text:
CS-E5480 Digital Ethics·Group Presentation
    Digital Piracy
    An Ethical Examination
    
      Piracy resists easy moral judgment. Depending on whose interests are
      foregrounded — the consumer priced out of fragmented platforms, the creator
      whose livelihood depends on compensation, or the buyer who discovers their
      "purchase" was only ever a licence — the same act can appear as a rational
      response, an ethical violation, or a symptom of a broken system. These three
      perspectives do not converge on a verdict; they map the terrain of a genuinely
      contested problem.
    
  

  
    
      
      Anonymous, A Spanish Galleon, mid-17th century engraving. Public domain.
    
  




  ☠ Chart the Course ☠


Three Perspectives


  
    I
    The Consumer's Perspective
    How fragmented streaming landscapes and rising subscription costs reframe piracy as a rational — if not justifiable — choice.
    Set Sail →
  
  
    II
    The Creator's Perspective
    Economic and moral rights under the Berne Convention, and what three philosophical frameworks reveal about the ethics of unpaid use.
    Set Sail →
  
  
    III
    Ownership and Licensing
    When clicking "Buy" delivers a revocable licence instead of property, the ethical calculus of piracy shifts in ways the law has not caught up with.
    Set Sail →

title: Digital Ownership
text: ##This whole page is a placeholder generated by Claude

title: Digital Ownership
text:
The ethical question
When you pay full price to buy something digital, can you truly own it — or are you just renting access until the seller decides otherwise? This section examines the gap between what “purchasing” digital media promises and what it actually delivers, and why that gap matters for the ethics of piracy.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Core thesis
Clicking “Buy” on a digital storefront hands you a revocable licence, not a property right. The Federal Trade Commission warned in 2024 that the word buy can be actively misleading when the seller retains the right to take access away (FTC, 2024). Most consumers don’t realise the swap from ownership to licencing has happened.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Rent vs. own




Subscription
Digital “purchase”
Physical copy




Pay once?
No
Yes
Yes


Revocable?
Yes
Yes
No


Survives platform shutdown?
No
No
Yes


Resell/lend?
No
No
Yes



Subscriptions are at least honest — you pay to keep access. One-time digital purchases borrow ownership’s vocabulary and price point while delivering something much closer to a rental.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Platform dependency & lock-in

Your library is tied to one platform’s app and account
You cannot move it to a competitor or export it
You cannot resell or lend individual items
If you leave the platform (or the platform leaves you), the library goes with it

All of these were taken for granted with physical media.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
When platforms die: The Crew
Ubisoft’s racing game The Crew was sold as a purchase to millions of players. Because it required a persistent server connection even for solo play, when Ubisoft shut those servers off on 31 March 2024, the game became completely unplayable — not degraded, not offline-only, simply gone.
French consumer group UFC-Que Choisir and a California class action both sued Ubisoft, alleging buyers had been misled into thinking they owned the game (Game Developer, 2024). The case attracted backing from the Stop Killing Games initiative, which had gathered over 1.3 million signatures arguing that publishers should not be allowed to permanently disable purchased software (Wikipedia, 2024).

title: Digital Ownership
text:
The legal mechanism: first-sale doctrine
Copyright law was designed around physical copies and included a consumer-protective balance: the first-sale doctrine, which lets you resell, lend, or give away a copy you lawfully bought. That is what makes second-hand bookshops and libraries legal.
Digital distribution exploits the gap. In Capitol Records v. ReDigi (2018), courts held that first-sale doctrine does not extend to digital files — transferring a file necessarily creates a new copy, which is the copyright holder’s exclusive right. Sellers then layer licence agreements on top of that gap, stripping consumers of resale and transfer rights without ever announcing the loss.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Counter-arguments
“The terms are disclosed — buyers agreed.” EULA terms are buried, unreadable, and non-negotiable. Nominal consent to a 50-page agreement written by the seller’s lawyers is not meaningful informed consent.
“DRM and always-online protect creators from piracy.” That may justify technical measures — it does not justify labelling a revocable licence as a purchase.
“Digital is cheaper and more convenient.” Convenience is a genuine benefit. Mislabelling the product is a separate issue.
“Always-online enables updates and anti-cheat.” Those are design choices with trade-offs. The harm is not the design choice itself but selling the result as permanent ownership.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Why it matters ethically

Honesty: consumers are charged ownership prices and given ownership language, but receive a revocable licence
Informed consent: the difference between what is sold and what is delivered is not communicated clearly
Preservation: when a paid-for library can be erased by a corporate decision, personal archiving starts to look less like theft and more like self-defence

This doesn’t make piracy morally right. But it does mean the moral weight that rights-holders invoke against piracy — the appeal to a fair exchange honestly entered into — is partly undermined by their own side of the bargain.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
References
Federal Trade Commission. (2024, April). Do you really own the digital items you paid for? https://consumer.ftc.gov/consumer-alerts/2024/04/do-you-really-own-digital-items-you-paid
Game Developer. (2024). French consumer group sues Ubisoft over shutdown of The Crew. https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/french-consumer-group-sues-ubisoft-over-shutdown-of-the-crew
Wikipedia. (2024). Stop Killing Games. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stop_Killing_Games
Wikipedia. (2018). Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capitol_Records,_LLC_v._ReDigi_Inc.

title: Project Plan: Digital Ethics Essay on Piracy
text:
Arguments for piracy: - Wider (equal) access to content in restricted areas (games banned in Australia, other censored content) - Wider distribution of content
Arguments against piracy: - Creators lose income - Are not incentivised to create as much content




Arguments for piracy: - Means for obtaining original efforts (manga, Blood, Taylor Swift catalogue) - Saves money (until costs become negligible to income) - Content fragmented across multiple platforms - Access to censored content and free media (Russia and China) - Archiving and saving information so that it doesn’t get forgotten (Anna’s Archive)
Arguments against piracy: - Unfair to people who follow the law (paying streaming fees) - Unregulated and risky: viruses and scams - Piracy sites need income too - Promote gambling





Renting (subscription) vs. owning
Media platform dependency: the media you purchase is locked into the platform
If a platform shuts down, what happens to the media you “own”?
Stop Killing Games initiative: online games can’t be played when providers shut down servers

Wikipedia: Stop Killing Games







What aspects of a media piece can you copyright, and what makes it distinct enough to be copyrightable?

Reference video

Does the size of the creator affect the morality of pirating? Is it okay to pirate from commercially successful companies vs. indie studios? Where is the limit?







Quarto website (main deliverable)
3000-4000 words
Minimum 5 credible sources (good mix)
Declare any AI usage
5-minute presentation of the website
Contribution statement






Three perspectives, each person takes one
Draft via pull requests
Write in bullet points initially
Deadline: 5 June 2026
